title: Iranian journalists in exile say Islamic regime could collapse in weeks | ABC NEWS
description: A group of Iranian journalists in exile say the Islamic regime is showing ‘no signs of recovery’ and could collapse within weeks. Established in 2017, Iran International is a media outlet opposed to the Iranian Revolutionary Guard and broadcasts to Farsi-speaking communities in Iran and around the world.  Presenter Fariba Shirzi and news director Aliasghar Ramezanpour hope the Israel-US operation against Tehran paves the way for their homecoming, but they also fear unpredictable outcomes while the regime nears its demise. title: The Iran soccer team is THE story of the Asian Cup
description: Iran's AFC Asian Cup is over, but the on field part of their story feels insignificant in the context of the players' broader situation. Confusion and fear surround the players who have been described as "wartime traitors" by powerful figures in the Middle East nation. Chaotic scenes outside their last game have underlined the concerns. Who is responsible for their safety and what fate awaits them once they leave Australia? We unpack a story that is towering over the soccer tournament. Featured: Amanda Shalala, senior reporter, ABC Sport.   To catch up on everything that’s making sports headlines recently, listen to more episodes of ABC Sport Daily,’ hosted by Patrick Stack on ABC listen or wherever you get your podcasts, and get in touch with them on social media via @abc_sport. title: The biggest market sell-off since 'Liberation Day' | The Business | ABC NEWS
description: The ASX has been hit with a sharp sell-off, as the Brent crude oil price skyrockets as much as 25 per cent to almost $US120 a barrel.  It's the Australian market's worst day since the "Liberation Day" sell-off in April 2025.  The ASX 200 closed down 2.9 per cent.  Wall Street tumbled after the continuation of Middle East hostilities sent oil prices rocketing and new data showed a sharp deterioration in the US jobs market.  AMP's deputy chief economist Diana Mousina says "markets are finally responding to news that this is probably not going to be resolved as quickly as they would have liked."  "It's not unusual for markets to act and behave like a deer in the headlights, waiting for the train to hit before everyone piles on", said Roger Montgomery from Montgomery Investment Management.  Meanwhile, the president of Chicago-based Northern Asset Trust Management, Michael Hunstad says financial markets don't expect a long-drawn out war.   Strikes on energy facilities across the Middle East have forced Gulf nations to scale back production of oil and gas, forcing up energy prices.  MST Financial senior energy analyst, Saul Kavonic thinks crude oil could reach $US150 per barrel.  From Asia to Australia's ASX 200, investors are dumping stocks as fears of another inflation spike and stagflation grow.   In Australia, motorists are already seeing higher fuel prices. Oxford Economics' Ben Udy says it's going to put more pressure on the Reserve Bank to start hiking interest rates.  Some petrol stations are scrambling to curb demand with one major retailer, United, even telling its wholesale customers it's suspending all allocations across all locations effective immediately as it reassesses supply and cargo. The government insists there's plenty of fuel supplies in the country and Australians shouldn't panic buy. #ABCBusiness Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE Read more here: https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-09/asx-markets-business-news-live-updates-march-9/106429652  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad.
